title-group.article-title: Biomarkers of organ stress and injury following the Boston Marathon

article-meta.aff: 1Department of Health, Human Performance and Recreation, University of Arkansas, Fayetteville, Arkansas, United States
2Institute for Exercise and Environmental Medicine, Texas Health Presbyterian Hospital Dallas, Dallas, Texas, United States
3Department of Internal Medicine, University of Texas Southwestern Medical Center, Dallas, Texas, United States
4Department of Health, Exercise and Sports Sciences, University of New Mexico, Albuquerque, New Mexico, United States
5Heat and Hydration Optimization (H2O) Lab, Exercise Science Research Center, Department of Health, Human Performance and Recreation, University of Arkansas, Fayetteville, Arkansas, United States
6Cardiology Division, Massachusetts General Hospital, Boston, Massachusetts, United States

fn.p: Z. J. McKenna and W. C. Atkins contributed equally to this work.

Z.J.M., W.C.A., C.L.B., and B.P.M. conceived and designed research; Z.J.M., W.C.A., C.L.B., X.Z., A.K.M., R.P., S.C.W., S.G., and B.P.M. performed experiments; Z.J.M., W.C.A., C.L.B., and B.P.M. analyzed data; Z.J.M., W.C.A., C.L.B., and B.P.M. interpreted results of experiments; Z.J.M. prepared figures; Z.J.M., W.C.A., and B.P.M. drafted manuscript; Z.J.M., W.C.A., C.L.B., X.Z., A.K.M., R.P., S.C.W., S.G., and B.P.M. edited and revised manuscript; Z.J.M., W.C.A., C.L.B., X.Z., A.K.M., R.P., S.C.W., S.G., and B.P.M. approved final version of manuscript.

license.license-p: Licensed under Creative Commons Attribution CC-BY-NC 4.0

abstract.p: We examined the changes in blood and urine-based biomarkers of organ stress/injury and hydration before and after the 2024 Boston Marathon. Our primary aims were to compare responses between males and females, and euhydrated and hypohydrated runners. Seventy-two runners (34 males/38 females; 50 ± 11 yr; finish time 3:45 ± 0:32 h:min) had blood and/or urine samples taken before and after the race. Average temperature on a race day was 19 ± 3°C, and relative humidity was 47 ± 15%. Samples were assayed for markers of hydration, intestinal injury (intestinal fatty acid binding protein and soluble cluster of differentiation 14), skeletal muscle damage (creatine kinase), and acute kidney injury (tissue inhibitor of metalloproteinase 2, insulin-like growth factor binding protein 7, neutrophil gelatinase-associated lipocalin, kidney injury molecule-1, creatinine). Markers of hydration were elevated from pre- to postrace, but only 25%–34% of runners were deemed hypohydrated postrace. All markers of organ stress/injury were increased postrace (fold change: 1.3–1455; P ≤ 0.007 for all indices); however, there were no differences based on sex or hydration status (P ≥ 0.099 for all indices). Our findings show that the Boston Marathon produces a significant level of stress on the gastrointestinal and renal organ systems and that these effects do not appear to be modulated by biological sex. In addition, our findings suggest that the substantial increases in markers of kidney and intestinal barrier injury persist even when severe hypohydration is prevented, suggesting that during the marathon, maintaining hydration cannot fully offset the risk for organ injury. These findings should be considered when designing interventions to minimize gastrointestinal and renal-related health complications associated with marathon running to promote both health and performance in recreational, but completive marathon runners.

abstract.title: NEW & NOTEWORTHY
abstract.p: We show that the Boston Marathon produces a significant level of stress on the gastrointestinal and renal organ systems, but these effects do not appear to be modulated by biological sex or age. Furthermore, the substantial rise in the markers of kidney and intestinal barrier injury persisted despite runners mitigating severe hypohydration. These findings may be useful for identifying interventions aimed at minimizing acute health complications associated with marathon running.

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: The marathon is a popular endurance event often regarded as the pinnacle of performance for endurance athletes. In general, the exercise training required to complete a marathon elicits numerous positive physiological benefits including improved metabolic, cardiovascular, and musculoskeletal health ultimately contributing to enhanced health and life span (1, 2). However, the prolonged exertion that occurs during a marathon event itself provides a significant acute stress that has the potential to cause pathophysiological responses in several organ systems (2). For example, acute cardiovascular, renal, gastrointestinal, and musculoskeletal disturbances have been documented in runners during marathon events (3-6). Although these alternations are most commonly transient and resolve without treatment, they have the potential to impact race performance and overtime may lead to negative health consequences. Thus, a comprehensive understanding of the contributing risk factors to the pathophysiological responses to marathon running is crucial to improve both performance and health of runners.

One of the most profound physiological responses to endurance exercise is the systemic alteration in blood flow. During exercise, perfusion to the working skeletal muscle is increased to support aerobic energy demand (7). This hemodynamic response is primarily mediated via local factors in the muscle that reduce vascular resistance as well as large increases in cardiac output. In addition, other vascular beds, such as the splanchnic and renal vascular beds, increase resistance to maintain adequate blood pressure (8). These hemodynamic changes can lead to splanchnic and renal hypoperfusion that may contribute to acute organ injury and thereby compromise essential organ system function (9-11). Furthermore, increased core temperature and dehydration associated with high levels of metabolic heat production and/or high environmental heat load exacerbate these responses as blood is sent to the skin to increase heat dissipation (12). In the gut, both hypoperfusion and hyperthermia can lead to intestinal barrier injury (9, 13), which may manifest as symptoms of gastrointestinal distress (i.e., nausea, bloating, and diarrhea) and perhaps contribute to more severe clinical complications such as exertional heat stroke (14). Likewise, dehydration, hyperthermia, and reduced renal perfusion during exercise can lead to transient increases in acute kidney injury risk (15). Although both intestinal barrier integrity and renal stress may contribute to the pathophysiological responses during marathon running, less is known about the specific roles of relevant biological factors, such as sex, that may make individuals more susceptible to increased risk for intestinal barrier injury and acute kidney injury risk.

The role of biological sex on gastrointestinal and renal responses to marathon running is not well established. Regarding the gastrointestinal responses, previous data are equivocal with some survey studies showing that female athletes report higher rates of exercise-induced gastrointestinal symptoms than their male counterparts (16), and others reporting no sex differences during endurance training (17). Furthermore, laboratory-based studies with high experimental control have provided no evidence of sex differences on gastrointestinal responses to endurance exercise (18), though these observations require confirmation in the field, where external validity is higher. Interestingly, experimental data indicate that females may be more resistant to ischemia-reperfusion-induced intestinal injury compared with males (19), and females appear to have a lower rise in biomarkers of end-organ damage following exertional heat stroke (20). Thus, there is some evidence to suggest that females may be protected against exercise-induced organ stress/injury. In support of this, observational studies suggest that males may be at increased risk for heat-related acute kidney injury, though the mechanisms underlying this potential difference are not understood (21, 22). We have previously shown that hydration behaviors and status may differ based on sex, which could lead to differences in both intestinal barrier and acute kidney injury responses (23). Given that females contribute to substantial number of marathon runners worldwide along with paucity of data on females following marathon events, there is a critical need to investigate the role of sex on these responses.

The Boston Marathon provides a unique natural laboratory to investigate these physiological responses, given its historical variability in environmental conditions, competitive field, and diverse population of runners. The Boston Marathon produces overall faster times compared with other races due to strict qualifying standards. Furthermore, since the course is downhill for the initial 16 km, muscle damage is inherent, which further increases the risk for acute kidney injury (24). We have previously shown consistent elevations in the markers of intestinal cell injury and acute kidney injury, with incomplete recovery 24 h following running (23, 25). However, the lack of baseline (i.e., prerace) data in our previous study makes it difficult to interpret the cross-race changes in these markers. Understanding the extent of gastrointestinal symptoms and the corresponding biomarker res-ponses during a competitive marathon setting, as well as the role that sex plays in these responses, may provide critical insights into strategies for mitigating exercise-induced organ injury and optimizing endurance performance.

Therefore, the purpose of this study was to examine changes in blood and urine-based biomarkers of organ stress and injury before and after the Boston Marathon. In addition, we compared responses between male and female runners to better understand the influence of sex on these responses. We also analyzed blood and urine samples for markers of hydration to determine the potential impacts of race-induced hypohydration on markers of end-organ injury/stress. Finally, we used secondary analyses to explore the impact of age (e.g., young vs. middle aged vs. older adults) and race finish time on baseline to postrace responses. We hypothesized that biomarkers of organ stress/injury would be elevated postrace regardless of sex, but that responses would be augmented in runners who became hypohydrated. The findings from this study may inform interventions aimed at minimizing pathophysiological complications associated with marathon running and enhancing endurance exercise tolerance in both recreational and elite athletes.

sec.title: METHODS
sec.p: Registered 2024 Boston Marathon runners were invited via an email sent by Boston Athletic Association race organizers to participate in this study. Interested runners contacted the study team and were sent a health history screening questionnaire to determine eligibility. Inclusion criteria required participants to have no previous vasovagal response during blood draws, be above the age of 18 yr, and had qualified to run the Boston Marathon based on age standards. Individuals were excluded if they had current cardiovascular, respiratory, gastrointestinal, bleeding, inflammatory, metabolic, fluid-electrolyte disorder, or other chronic disease. Individuals using selective cyclooxygenase-2 inhibitors, anti-hypertensives, or other medications that affect kidney, gastrointestinal, or cardiovascular function, or fluid-electrolyte balance were excluded. Elite runners and nonqualifying runners (e.g., charity runner) were not included in the study due to the large variability in potential finish times. This study was approved by the University of Arkansas Institutional Review Board, and participants provided written informed consent before participation in the study.

On the two days preceding the race, during athlete check-in at the Marathon Expo, participants reported to a designated research area for baseline data collection. After arrival and check-in, participants completed a research questionnaire, medical history form, and a baseline blood sample was collected. Baseline blood samples were collected during the Marathon Expo to avoid logistical concerns with collecting blood samples immediately before the start of the race (i.e., time constraints with collecting a large number of blood samples in a short period of time). Researchers provided verbal instructions for the procedures of data collection and familiarized participants with the research areas at both the start and finish lines. Researchers were available to answer any questions regarding study procedures for race day. Participants were provided with a paper copy of reminders.

On race day, participants reported to a designated research area in Hopkinton (near the start line) to provide a baseline urine sample. Immediately after completing the race, participants reported to the designated research area located in the medical tent near the finish line. The participants were asked to complete a brief postmarathon survey to assess symptomology and provide a urine and blood sample.

Gastrointestinal symptoms experienced during the race were assessed immediately postrace using a standardized recall questionnaire. Runners scored the symptoms experienced during the race on a 10-point scale (0 = no problems at all and 9 = the worst it has ever been). Questions included those specific to upper (reflux/heart burn, belching, bloating, stomach pain/cramps, vomiting, and nausea) and lower abdominal problems (intestinal cramps, flatulence, urge to defecate, left and right abdominal pain/stitch, loose stool, and diarrhea). These 13 questions were summed to provide a total gastrointestinal symptom score with a maximum score of 117. In addition, the questionnaire contained questions about other systemic problems such as dizziness, headache, muscle cramps, urge to urinate, thirst, fever, swelling, fatigue, muscle soreness/weakness, and tingling in arms or legs.

sec.title: Sample Analysis
sec.p: At pre- and postrace, participants were provided with a sealed urine sample cup and instructed to go to a nearby restroom to collect a sample. Urine samples were aliquoted, and we measured urine specific gravity shortly after collection (within 4–5 h) using a refractometer according to the manufacturer’s instructions (Master-Sur; Atago company Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). Remaining aliquots were stored at −80°C for later analyses. In addition, blood samples were collected through venipuncture of an arm vein into heparin, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), or serum separator Vacutainers in the seated upright position following at least 10 min of seated rest at the Marathon Expo (baseline) and at after race completion (postrace). Whole blood was analyzed immediately with i-STAT CHEM 8+ cartridges (Abbott Laboratories, IL) and then placed on ice and transferred to a local laboratory for centrifugation and storage. Blood samples were centrifuged within 2 h of collection to isolate plasma/serum, and aliquots were stored at −80°C for later analyses. Frozen urine and blood samples were shipped overnight on dry ice to the laboratory for follow-up analyses.

Frozen urine and plasma samples were thawed and briefly vortexed, and osmolality was assessed in duplicate using a single-sample freeze point osmometer (Model 3250; Advanced Instruments, Norwood, MA). To assess muscle damage, we measured serum creatine kinase (CK) (Medica RA chemistry analyzer). We measured plasma (heparin) intestinal fatty acid binding protein [I-FABP; intraassay coefficient of variation (CV) = 5%; 50-fold dilution] and plasma (EDTA) soluble cluster of differentiation 14 (sCD14; intraassay CV = 4%; 350-fold dilution) using enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (R&D Systems, Minneapolis, MN). I-FABP, a cytosolic protein present in mature enterocytes located in the duodenum/jejunum, was used to detect intestinal cell injury. sCD14 was used as an indirect marker of microbial translocation, as this protein is involved in the trafficking of lipopolysaccharides to immune cells. Plasma and serum biomarkers were corrected for changes in plasma volume (26).

Frozen urine samples were assayed for tissue inhibitor of metalloproteinase 2 (TIMP-2; intraassay CV = 3%; 50-fold dilution) and insulin like growth factor binding protein 7 (IGFBP7; intraassay CV = 4%; 25-fold dilution) (RayBiotech Life, Peachtree Corners, GA), as well as neutrophil gelatinase-associated lipocalin (NGAL; intraassay CV = 14%; 20-fold dilution) and kidney injury molecule-1 (KIM-1; intraassay CV = 13%; twofold dilution) via enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (R&D Systems, Minneapolis, MN). TIMP-2 and IGFBP7 are cell cycle arrest proteins, NGAL is released from kidney tubular cells under stress, and KIM-1 marker is released from the proximal tubule under stress; thus, these values were used to indicate acute kidney injury risk. The arithmetic product of TIMP-2 and IGFBP7 (TIMP-2 × IGFBP7) was used as the primary indication for acute kidney injury risk. Urinary biomarkers were normalized to urine specific gravity using the Levine–Feyh equation with a reference urine specific gravity of 1.020 to account for potential changes in urine concentration (27).

sec.title: Statistical Analyses
sec.p: Before statistical analyses, data were assessed to confirm that they met model assumptions (i.e., normality and equality of variance), and the data were inspected for influential data points. Due to a variety of factors, we were unable to obtain a sufficient quantity of blood or urine at baseline or postrace for some participants. Since these missing data were missing at random, they were excluded from final analyses, and the number of participants included for each variable is indicated in text, tables, and figures, where appropriate. We compared baseline or prerace and postrace data using paired sample t tests (two-tailed). We used unpaired t tests (two-tailed) to compare responses between males and females, as well as between euhydrated and hypohydrated runners. Blood and urine markers of hydration were analyzed using linear mixed-effects models with main effects of time (within factor) and sex (between factor; males vs. females) or hydration status (between factor; euhydrated vs. hypohydration). To explore the impact of age (young adults: 18–39 yr; middle aged: 40–59 yr; and older adults: ≥60 yr) on the baseline or prerace to postrace change in biomarkers, we performed secondary grouped analyses using one-way ANOVA (or Welch’s ANOVA in the case of unequal variances). We chose to categorize age in 20-year increments to allow for a sufficient number of runners in each age group, whereas still characterizing individuals as young, middle aged, or older aligning closely with the Boston Marathon age group qualifying times. We compared symptoms reported during the race by males and females and euhydrated and hypohydrated runners using the nonparametric Wilcoxon signed-ranked tests (two-tailed). In addition, we explored the relations between finish time and organ stress and injury markers as well as gastrointestinal symptoms and markers of intestinal barrier injury using simple linear regressions. Statistical analyses were performed in Prism 10 (GraphPad Software Inc., La Jolla, CA). Data in text, tables, and figures are presented as means ± standard deviations and [minimum–maximum], where appropriate. Data analyzed using nonparametric tests are shown as median [minimum–maximum]. Statistical significance was set a priori to P < 0.05. Clinical significance was set as follows: for IFABP to a change from baseline to postrace >1,000 pg/mL (28); for IGFBP7 × TMIP2, clinical significance was set as a postrace value >0.30 (ng/mL)2/1,000 (29); and for creatinine a change of 0.30 mg/dL. (30). In addition, hydration status was evaluated using the following criteria to determine hypohydration: urine specific gravity > 1.020 and/or plasma osmolality > 300 mosmol/kgH2O (31, 32).

sec.title: Participants and Race Conditions
sec.p: A total of 72 runners (34 males and 38 females) completed this study. However, we were only able to collect and analyze complete (i.e., baseline or prerace and postrace) blood and/or urine samples on a total of 60 participants. The specific sample sizes for each variable are shown in the tables/figures. Participant characteristics including age, height, weight, and race finish time are shown in Table 1. Average age was 34 ± 4 yr (7 males and 11 females) for the young cohort, 55 ± 2 yr (13 males and 21 females) for the middle-aged cohort, and 64 ± 3 yr (14 males and 6 females) for the older cohort. Female race finish time was ~9% longer compared with males (P = 0.015). Environmental conditions reported every hour in Boston, MA, on the race day are shown in Table 2. The average air temperature on the race day was 19 ± 3°C, relative humidity was 47 ± 15%, which collectively yielded an estimated wet bulb temperature of 12 ± 1°C and heat index of 18 ± 3°C.

sec.title: Markers of Hydration Status
sec.p: Detailed markers of hydration and electrolyte balance are shown in Table 3. We noted pre- to postrace changes in blood sodium (1 ± 3 mEq/L), chloride (2 ± 4 mEq/L), ionized calcium (−0.1 ± 0.1 mmol/L), plasma osmolality (6 ± 8 mosmol/kgH2O), and urine specific gravity (0.008 ± 0.016 AU) (main effects of time: P < 0.001 for all indices; Table 3). Males had higher overall blood sodium (main effect of sex: P = 0.046) and plasma osmolality (main effect of sex: P = 0.008) compared with females. Twenty-five percent of runners (8 males and 10 females) had a postrace urine specific gravity >1.020, and 34% of runners (16 males and 8 females) had a postrace plasma osmolality >300 mosmol/kgH2O.

sec.title: Markers of Acute Kidney Injury Risk
sec.p: Urinary biomarkers of acute kidney injury are shown in Fig. 1, whereas blood-based markers of renal stress are shown in Table 3. There were pre- to postrace increases in urine TIMP-2 × IGFBP7 [Δ18 ± 29 (ng/mL)2/1,000; P < 0.001], NGAL (Δ29 ± 45 ng/mL; P < 0.001), and KIM-1 (Δ2.5 ± 2.5 ng/mL; P < 0.001). However, there were no differences in the pre- to postrace changes between males and females or between euhydrated and hypohydrated runners for urinary markers of acute kidney injury (Fig. 1). Likewise, exploratory analyses showed no differences between young, middle aged, and older adults (Fig. 2). Creatinine and blood urea nitrogen increased from baseline to postrace (main effects of time: P < 0.001), and as expected, both creatinine and blood urea nitrogen were higher in males compared with females (main effects of sex: P < 0.001; Table 3). Fifty-three of the 60 (88%) runners with urine samples included in this study had a postrace TIMP-2 × IGFBP7 above the 0.30 (ng/mL)2/1,000 threshold, and 64 of the 67 (96%) runners with blood samples showed baseline to postrace increases in creatinine ≥0.30 mg/dL. Race finish time did not predict the change in TIMP-2 × IGFBP7 (P = 0.825), NGAL (P = 0.298), or KIM-1 (P = 0.182).

sec.title: Markers of Intestinal Barrier Injury, Muscle Damage, and Symptoms
sec.p: IFABP (Δ4,379 ± 4,566 pg/mL; P < 0.001), sCD14 (Δ493 ± 1,362 pg/mL; P = 0.007), and creatine kinase (Δ237 ± 193 U/L; P < 0.001) all increased from baseline to postrace (Fig. 3). However, there were no differences in the baseline to postrace changes between males and females or euhydrated and hypohydrated runners (Fig. 3). Forty-five of the 60 runners in the study (75%) had baseline to postrace increases in IFABP >1,000 pg/mL. Symptoms reported by runners during the race are summarized in Table 4. There were no differences in the total gastrointestinal symptom score between males and females (P = 0.750) or between euhydrated and hypohydrated runners. Race finish time did not predict the change in IFABP (P = 0.872), sCD14 (P = 0.054), or CK (P = 0.907). We did not observe a linear relation between total gastrointestinal symptoms scores and the baseline to postrace change in IFABP (P = 0.138) or sCD14 (P = 0.522).

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: The key findings from this study were as follows: 1) there were substantial rises in several markers of organ injury following the marathon indicating a significant level of stress on the gastrointestinal and renal systems; 2) we observed no differences between male and female responses for any of the measured variables adding to the growing body of evidence, indicating that sex may not be a contributing factor to increases in organ stress observed during endurance exercise; importantly, we highlight that our cohort is a representative of competitive yet nonelite marathon runners; 3) contrary to previous reports, many runners were able to successfully mitigate dehydration that resulted in only minor increases in urine- and plasma-based markers of hydration with no significant electrolyte imbalances; 4) despite this maintenance in hydration, runners still experienced substantial increases in markers of acute kidney and intestinal barrier injury. In fact, there were no differences in biomarker responses between runners who remained euhydrated and those who became hypohydrated during the race. Thus, although hypohydration clearly plays a role in exacerbating acute organ stress, maintaining hydration cannot fully offset risk for organ injury during a marathon event; and 5) no clear differences were observed in biomarker responses between young, middle aged, and older adults.

The baseline to postrace increases in markers of organ stress/injury is consistent with previous data collectively showing that marathon running contributes to acute and likely transient stress on the gastrointestinal and renal organ systems (23, 25, 33-41). For example, we previously reported elevated IFABP following the Boston Marathon, with values decreasing 24 h postrace (25). However, since our previous study did not establish baseline levels, we were unable to determine the acute impact of the marathon on these responses. The current study extends upon those findings showing clear baseline to postrace increases in IFABP and sCD14 that collectively indicate intestinal barrier injury. However, we did not observe significant relations between markers of intestinal barrier injury and reported symptomology, which corroborates previous field studies and suggests that intestinal barrier injury may not be the driving cause of gastrointestinal symptoms reported during endurance events (25, 40). Furthermore, we report clear and consistent rises in several markers of acute injury with values far exceeding the typical threshold for clinical significance. For example, 88% of runners in this study had a TIMP-2 × IGFBP7 above the 0.30 (ng/mL)2/1,000 threshold postrace, and likewise 96% of runners showed baseline to postrace increases in creatinine ≥0.30 mg/dL. Importantly, there appears to be a good agreement between blood and urine-based markers of acute kidney injury risk, which suggests that these markers can be used in conjunction when assessing renal stress following endurance exercise. The mechanisms underlying the increases in acute kidney injury biomarkers are multifactorial but likely include hypoperfusion associated with prolonged endurance exercise, hyperthermia secondary to metabolic heat production and high environmental temperatures, hypohydration, and perhaps muscle damage associated with sustained eccentric loading. Given the race conditions for this specific race and the overall challenging Boston Marathon course, these responses may be on the higher end compared with what may be observed in other races performed in cooler environments or with less downhill running. Nonetheless, although we have previously shown that these values mostly recover in the 24 h following the race (23, 25), we emphasize that the acute organ stress (both intestinal and kidney) should be considered during the recovery process, especially when races are performed in warmer climates.

Previous studies have shown that males and females differ in their biomarker responses to heat stroke and ischemia-reperfusion stress (19, 20). Furthermore, epidemiological data indicate that males have higher rates of heat-related acute kidney injury compared with females (21, 22). Although the underlying physiological mechanisms are not understood, these previous studies have provided some evidence that females may be protected against exercise- and/or heat-induced organ injury. Despite these reports, we revealed no sex-based differences for any of the measured variables including in markers of hydration, intestinal barrier injury, acute kidney injury, or skeletal muscle damage. Likewise, we did not observe differences in subjective symptoms of gastrointestinal distress during the race. Our data corroborate Snipe et al. (18), who found no sex differences in markers of intestinal barrier injury following endurance exercise. Thus, it seems that sex may not be a primary factor to the increases in organ stress observed during prolonged endurance exercise. Importantly, we highlight that our study comprises a representative sample of males and female runners to draw conclusions. For example, we studied a similar number of males and female runners, who were of similar age range, as well as sex- and age-specific running time [i.e., consistent with the literature females finish times were ~10% longer than males (42)].

There is considerable variability in hypohydration rates following endurance events, perhaps due to varying levels of experience or race finish times (23, 36, 37, 43, 44). Herein, we report significant increases in pre- to postrace urine specific gravity and plasma osmolality, but not urine osmolality. However, it is important to highlight that despite these significant increases, all the measured hydration biomarkers still fell within the normal range postrace, suggesting that runners experienced only mild, if any, levels of hypohydration. This was somewhat surprising given that the environmental conditions on race day (i.e., average heat index of 18 ± 3°C) were sufficient to induce a thermal challenge. Perhaps, our participants were better equipped to handle cross-race dehydration (i.e., due to previous race experience). Nonetheless, we show that runners still experienced increases in markers of acute kidney injury risk regardless of their postrace hydration status. For example, we report substantial increases in urinary TIMP-2 × IGFBP7, NGAL, and KIM-1, which collectively indicate some level of transient kidney injury. In the context of fluid shifts, it is important to interpret these biomarkers with potential changes in urine concentration. To account for this potential confounding factor, we normalized urinary markers to urine specific gravity. Our findings show that the substantial rises in all markers of kidney injury persisted after correcting for changes in urine specific gravity, further demonstrating that the risk for kidney injury is mediated by several contributing factors beyond severe hypohydration.

There are several pieces of practical information from this study that the scientific and running community should consider moving forward. Although preventing dehydration is clearly important for a variety of health and performance metrics, it does not fully prevent the increase in these markers which is likely driven by sustained hypoperfusion and perhaps hyperthermia. Thus, runners should consider recovery postrace to limit the repeated stress on these organ systems. Our study provides no evidence that males and females respond inherently different to the marathon. Likewise, we did not observe differences between age groups, and our exploratory findings show that running time was not a significant predictor of the biomarker responses postrace. Thus, individuals should consider their unique physiology as there is not consistent evidence to suggest that recommendations should be sex- or age-specific as these factors alone may not be a reliable determinant of needs. A one-size-fits-all approach based on sex or age is insufficient and underscores the important of individualized strategies. Furthermore, even in cases of mild or no apparent hypohydration, we observed increases in measured biomarkers, suggesting that subtle fluid deficits combined with the marathon imposes physiological stress. This raises the possibility that without proactive and personalized fluid replacement strategies, greater or prolonged increases in dehydration could lead to greater increases in these biomarkers.

This study was not without limitation. Baseline blood samples were not collected immediately before the race; thus, day-to-day variation in the biomarkers may have some impact on our results. Similarly, given that this study was conducted in the field, blood and urine samples were processed as soon as it was logistically feasible (within ~2 h of collection); however, we cannot rule out the potential for sample degradation due to delayed sample processing, which may have underestimated the concentrations of some biomarkers (e.g., sCD14). Although we provide a wide range of organ injury/stress biomarkers, we do not have comprehensive physiological or nutrition data, which limit our ability to make definitive conclusions regarding the mechanisms of organ injury. Although such data would provide important insights, it is logistically challenging for a number of reasons including cost and issues with participant adherence. However, considering the growing interest and development in wearable biosensors, these data are becoming increasingly more realistic to obtain in a wide range of runners. Likewise, we did not obtain comprehensive assessments of training status or volume in the weeks/months leading up to the race, nor were we able to collect inter-race fueling and hydration metrics. These data would help determine if specific prerace preparation (e.g., downhill or heat-focused training, etc.) or perhaps certain in-race strategies (low vs. high carbohydrate fueling) could help mitigate the increase in these biomarkers. Future work should explore these options when conducting similar field-based studies.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: In conclusion, our data demonstrate that the Boston Marathon produces a significant level of stress on the gastrointestinal and renal organ systems and that these effects do not appear to be modulated by biological sex. In addition, our findings suggest that the substantial increases in markers of kidney and intestinal barrier injury persist even when severe hypohydration is prevented, suggesting that during the marathon, maintaining hydration cannot fully offset risk for organ injury. These findings should be considered when considering interventions aimed at minimizing various gastrointestinal and renal-related health complications associated with marathon running to promote both health and performance in recreational, but completive marathon runners.

ack.title: ACKNOWLEDGMENTS
ack.p: We thank Tremell Allen and Marissa Nebel for their assistance with data collection. We thank Michael Malone and Trina Goodale for their assistance with postrace blood collection. We thank Chris Troyanos and the entire BAA medical directors for facilitating logistics related to recruitment, parking, and lab space availability at the Expo, start and finish lines. We thank Dr. Meagan Wasfy for providing lab space for sample analysis and temporary storage. Finally, we thank the athletes who participated in this study for their time and effort. Graphical Abstract made with a licensed version of BioRender.com.

sec.title: GRANTS
sec.p: This study was funded by the Boston Athletic Association Medical Research Grant Program. Z.J.M. was supported by American Heart Association Grant (23POST1023065). W.C.A. was supported by American Heart Association Grant (24POST1192222).

fn.p: No conflicts of interest, financial or otherwise, are declared by the authors.

sec.title: DATA AVAILABILITY
sec.p: Data will be made available upon reasonable request.

ref.mixed-citation: PredelHG. Marathon run: cardiovascular adaptation and cardiovascular risk. Eur Heart J 35: 3091–3098, 2014. doi:10.1093/eurheartj/eht502.24408890

BraschlerL, NikolaidisPT, ThuanyM, ChlíbkováD, RosemannT, WeissK, WilhelmM, KnechtleB. Physiology and pathophysiology of marathon running: a narrative review. Sports Med Open 11: 10, 2025. doi:10.1186/s40798-025-00810-3.39871014 PMC11772678

caption.p: Markers of acute kidney injury risk at pre- and postrace. Data are shown as means ± standard deviation with individual data. Urine biomarkers were normalized to urine specific gravity using the Levine–Feyh equation with a reference urine specific gravity of 1.020. Cutoffs for euhydrated (EuH) and hypohydrated (HyH) were urine specific gravity of 1.020 or plasma osmolality of 300 mosmol/kgH2O. Data were analyzed using either paired or unpaired t tests (two-tailed). n = 30 males; n = 30 females; n = 24 euhydrated; n = 36 hypohydrated. IGFBP7, insulin-like growth factor binding protein 7; KIM-1, kidney injury molecule-1; NGAL, neutrophil gelatinase-associated lipocalin; TIMP-2, tissue inhibitor of metalloproteinase 2.

Baseline or prerace to postrace changes in biomarkers of organ stress/injury across young (18–39 yr), middle aged (40–59 yr), and older (≥60 yr) adults. Data are shown as means ± standard deviation with individual data. Plasma and serum biomarkers were corrected for changes in plasma volume. Urine biomarkers were normalized to urine specific gravity using the Levine–Feyh equation with a reference urine specific gravity of 1.020. Data were analyzed using one-way ANOVA. Blood biomarkers (IFABP, sCD14, and CK): n = 15, 18–39 yr; n = 29, 40–59 yr; n = 16, ≥60 yr. Urine biomarkers (TIMP-2 × IGFBP7, NGAL, and KIM-1): n = 15, 18–39 yr; n = 30, 40–59 yr; n = 15, ≥60 yr. CK, creatine kinase; IFABP, intestinal fatty acid binding protein; IGFBP7, insulin-like growth factor binding protein 7; KIM-1, kidney injury molecule-1; NGAL, neutrophil gelatinase-associated lipocalin; sCD14, soluble cluster of differentiation 14; TIMP-2, tissue inhibitor of metalloproteinase 2.

Markers of intestinal barrier injury and skeletal muscle damage at baseline and postrace. Data are shown as means ± standard deviation with individual data. Plasma and serum biomarkers were corrected for changes in plasma volume. Cutoffs for euhydrated (EuH) and hypohydrated (HyH) were urine specific gravity of 1.020 or plasma osmolality of 300 mosmol/kgH2O. Data were analyzed using either paired or unpaired t tests (two-tailed). n = 31 males; n = 29 females; n = 24 euhydrated; n = 36 hypohydrated. CK, creatine kinase; IFABP, intestinal fatty acid binding protein; sCD14, soluble cluster of differentiation 14.

fn.p: Data are means ± standard deviation [minimum–maximum]. Females reported post- or perimenopausal, n = 6; no birth control, n = 18; oral contraceptive, n = 2; copper or hormonal intrauterine device, n = 10; and hysterectomy, n = 2. Data between males and females as well as between euhydrated and hypohydrated runners were compared with unpaired t tests (two-tailed). Bolded values indicate P < 0.05.

caption.p: Environmental conditions in Boston, MA on race day (15 April 2024)
Blood and urine analysis at baseline or prerace and postrace

fn.p: Baseline samples were collected in two days before the race, whereas prerace samples were collected just before the start of the race. Data were analyzed using linear mixed-effects models with main effects of time, sex, and the time by sex interaction and are shown as means ± standard deviation. BUN, blood urea nitrogen; USG, urine specific gravity. Bolded values indicate P < 0.05.

caption.p: Subjective symptoms experienced during the race

fn.p: Total GI symptom score is the total amount of symptoms reported across 13 different areas on a 10-point scale (0 = no problems at all and 9 = the worst it has ever been) with a maximum score of 117. Data between males and females as well as between euhydrated and hypohydrated runners were compared with Wilcoxon signed-ranked tests (two-tailed). Data are shown as median [minimum–maximum].
